did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including architectures like ConvNeXtLarge, DenseNet201, and MobileNetV2, to classify skin cancer (melanoma, BCC, etc.) using dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from training and evaluating 16 CNN architectures on the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets to classify skin cancer. It includes specific methodology (hybrid sampling strategy), hyperparameters, and quantitative performance metrics (e.g., ConvNeXtLarge recall of 82.76%), indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Identification of Skin Cancer Using Deep Learning
journal: 22nd International Conference on Electrical Engineering/Electronics, Computer, Telecommunications and Information Technology (ECTI-CON)
brief_summary: This study investigates the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for skin cancer classification using dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets. To address class imbalance, the authors employed a hybrid sampling strategy involving oversampling of malignant classes and undersampling of benign classes. Sixteen pre-trained CNN architectures, including ConvNeXtLarge, DenseNet201, and MobileNetV2, were evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and MCC. The experimental results demonstrated that ConvNeXtLarge achieved the highest overall performance with a recall of 82.76% for melanoma. Applying the resampling method significantly improved the recall of cancerous classes across all models, highlighting the effectiveness of balancing techniques in enhancing diagnostic sensitivity.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant class; deadliest form of skin cancer; oversampled to 1,000 images per class using SMOTE.
Malignant class; oversampled to 1,000 images per class using SMOTE.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Actinic Keratosis and Intraepithelial Carcinoma / Bowen's Disease

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratoses/Bowen's Disease, and a merged Benign class)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is to apply a 'deep learning-based classification model for skin cancer' and evaluates models on 'skin lesion classification'. The task involves distinguishing between malignant and benign classes using dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 and ISIC datasets. Performance was measured using classification metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and MCC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine 10000)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, obtained from the ISIC challenge. It contains images of various skin ailments including benign and malignant lesions.
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease (akiec)

use.training: Used for training 16 pre-trained CNN models in the first phase without data augmentation, and later for retraining models on a balanced dataset.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter tuning and preventing overfitting during model development.
use.testing: Used for performance evaluation and comparison of different models.
use.other: The dataset was partitioned at a ratio of 70:20:10 for training, validation, and testing respectively. In the second phase, malignant classes were oversampled to 1,000 images each using SMOTE, and benign classes were merged and undersampled to 3,000 images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: Public dataset containing dermatoscopic images used in conjunction with HAM10000. The paper references the ISIC challenge and dataset sources [23][24] as the origin of the images.
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease (akiec)

use.training: Combined with HAM10000 to form the total dataset of 10,015 dermoscopic images used for training.
use.validation: Combined with HAM10000 to form the total dataset used for validation.
use.testing: Combined with HAM10000 to form the total dataset used for testing.
use.other: The specific split between HAM10000 and ISIC contributions to the 10,015 total images is not explicitly detailed, but they are treated as the combined source for the study.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, MCC) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000_ISIC_Baseline_Phase1.description: Evaluation of 16 pre-trained CNN models on the original imbalanced dataset (7 classes) without data augmentation. Dataset split: 70% train, 20% validation, 10% test.
HAM10000_ISIC_Baseline_Phase1.notes: ConvNeXtLarge achieved the highest overall performance. Class imbalance remained a significant challenge, particularly for underrepresented cancerous lesions.

HAM10000_ISIC_Balanced_Phase2.description: Evaluation of the top 5 models from Phase 1 retrained on a balanced dataset. Balancing strategy: Malignant classes (mel, bcc, akiec) oversampled to 1,000 images each via SMOTE; Benign classes merged and undersampled to 3,000 images.
HAM10000_ISIC_Balanced_Phase2.notes: The hybrid sampling strategy significantly improved recall for cancerous classes, particularly melanoma, across all models. VGG16 showed the most significant relative improvement in melanoma recall.

architecture_used.main_model: ConvNeXtLarge (Primary), DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, EfficientNetV2B0, VGG16, EfficientNetV2S, EfficientNetB7, VGG19, Xception, ResNet152V2, InceptionV3, NASNetLarge, ResNet50V2, EfficientNetV2L, ConvNeXtXLarge, ConvNeXtBase

architecture_details.implementation_details: Models were used to extract image features, which were subsequently fed into classification layers. No new network was built from scratch; existing pre-trained models were utilized.

training_details.data_augmentation: None in Phase 1; Hybrid sampling strategy (SMOTE oversampling of malignant classes to 1,000 images each; merging and undersampling of benign classes to 3,000 images) in Phase 2

classification_or_output_head.details: Image features extracted by CNNs were fed into classification layers to predict skin lesion classes. Specific head architecture details (e.g., number of neurons, activation functions) are not explicitly reported beyond the use of standard classification layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Original images (600x450 pixels) were preprocessed to specific input shapes for different architectures: 224x224x3 (standard), 299x299x3 (InceptionV3, Xception), 331x331x3 (NASNetLarge), and 384x384x3 (EfficientNetV2S, EfficientNetV2L, EfficientNetB7).

Data Balancing (Sampling): A hybrid sampling strategy was applied in Phase 2 to address class imbalance. Malignant classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses) were oversampled to 1,000 images per class using SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique). Benign classes (benign keratosis-like lesions, dermatofibroma, melanocytic nevi, vascular lesions) were merged into a single class and undersampled to 3,000 images via random undersampling.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: In the first phase of the experiment, 16 pre-trained models were developed and evaluated without applying any data augmentation techniques.

Used in the second phase for class balancing. Malignant classes (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses) were oversampled to 1,000 images per class. SMOTE creates new samples by interpolating existing ones, selecting an instance from the minority class and its nearest neighbors, and producing synthetic data along the line segments between them.

Used in the second phase for class balancing. The benign class (merged from benign keratosis-like lesions, dermatofibroma, melanocytic nevi, and vascular lesions) was undersampled to 3,000 images to prevent class imbalance from skewing the model's learning process while maintaining diversity in lesion appearance.

conclusions: ConvNeXtLarge achieved the highest overall performance among the 16 tested CNN architectures, with an accuracy of 83.17% and an MCC of 0.662 on the imbalanced dataset.
The application of a hybrid sampling strategy (oversampling malignant classes and undersampling/merging benign classes) significantly improved model sensitivity to underrepresented malignant lesions.

Specifically, the recall for melanoma increased across all evaluated models after applying the resampling method, with ConvNeXtLarge's melanoma recall rising from 52.72% to 56.36% and VGG16's recall increasing from 26.36% to 52.9%.
Class imbalance remained a significant challenge in the baseline phase, particularly for underrepresented cancerous lesions, resulting in lower sensitivity for malignant cases in unbalanced models.

The study demonstrates that balancing techniques are effective in enhancing diagnostic sensitivity for skin cancer classification, addressing the limitations of imbalanced datasets in real-world medical applications.
important_remarks: The study merges four distinct benign classes (bkl, df, nv, vasc) into a single 'benign' category, which limits the model's ability to differentiate between specific benign lesion types and reduces clinical granularity.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between models; results are based on descriptive metrics only.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) for balancing; while the paper states evaluation was on the original test set, the synthetic samples generated during training could potentially influence validation if not strictly isolated, though the text implies the test set remained untouched.

The dataset is derived from HAM10000 and ISIC, which may have demographic biases (e.g., overrepresentation of fair-skinned populations) not explicitly analyzed in the paper, limiting generalizability to diverse skin types.
No external validation was performed; the study relies solely on the internal test split of the HAM10000/ISIC dataset, raising concerns about real-world generalizability.
The study evaluates 16 pre-trained CNN architectures but does not compare them against state-of-the-art models beyond those included in the benchmark set, potentially missing newer architectures.

The paper acknowledges that class imbalance is a significant challenge but addresses it via sampling rather than algorithmic adjustments, which may not reflect real-world prevalence rates in deployment scenarios.
The input image resolution was standardized to 224x224x3 for most models, which may discard fine-grained diagnostic features present in the original 600x450 pixel dermatoscopic images.
